Title: Building Voice AI for People Who Don’t Trust AI
Abstract:
Most AI products assume users are comfortable installing apps, creating accounts, and talking to chatbots. Many people are not.

In this talk, a founding engineer building production voice-first AI systems explores what happens when you remove the app entirely and let users interact with an LLM agent through a normal phone call.

The session focuses on designing conversational AI for audiences often ignored by mainstream AI products: people who are skeptical of AI, uncomfortable with modern apps, or simply unwilling to learn another interface.

Rather than treating the model itself as the main challenge, the talk explores the harder problem of building trust. Pavel shares practical lessons from deploying real-world voice agents, including conversation design, failure handling, latency expectations, speech interaction patterns, and why familiar interfaces like phone calls can dramatically lower adoption barriers.

Attendees will leave with a grounded perspective on voice AI systems, along with practical ideas for building AI products that feel more accessible, trustworthy, and human for everyday users.

Title: Shipping AI Features That Don’t Break in Production
Abstract:
LLM demos are easy. Shipping AI features that real users can depend on is where the hard engineering starts.

In this talk, an Android engineer working on AI-driven mobile features at Revolut shares lessons from building production AI products at scale, alongside developing an independent AI coaching app for padel players.

The session focuses on the messy reality behind AI integrations in mobile apps: latency, inference costs, hallucinations, unreliable outputs, rate limits, and the challenge of designing UX around systems that are fundamentally non-deterministic.

Instead of focusing on ideal demo flows, the talk explores what happens when the model gives incomplete, wrong, delayed, or unusable responses, and how engineering teams can build proper safeguards, fallbacks, and user experiences around those failures.

Attendees will leave with practical patterns, architectural decisions, and product lessons for making LLM-powered mobile features more stable, predictable, and usable in production environments.

Title: Agentic AI for Autonomous Compliance in Enterprise Tax Systems Using LLM-Driven Tax-to-Code Pipelines
Abstract: Modern enterprise tax systems operating across multinational jurisdictions face a growing challenge in translating continuously evolving legal regulations into executable compliance logic. Traditional compliance workflows rely heavily on manual interpretation of statutory text, resulting in update cycles that can take months and expose organizations to significant compliance drift and financial risk. This talk presents an LLM-driven Autonomous Compliance Infrastructure (ACI) that applies agentic AI principles to automate the end-to-end transformation of regulatory changes into deployable tax logic. The architecture combines Legal NLP, multi-agent orchestration, formal rule modeling, and a Tax-to-Code Engine that separates declarative regulatory policy from core application logic. The session focuses on practical system design decisions, explainability mechanisms, human-in-the-loop governance, and deployment validation strategies used to ensure reliability in legally critical environments. It also covers lessons learned while integrating large language models into enterprise-grade compliance workflows, including uncertainty handling, rollback strategies, observability, and scalability challenges. Attendees will gain insight into how agentic LLM systems can reduce compliance update cycles from months to hours while maintaining governance, auditability, and operational trust in real-world enterprise platforms.

Title: AMMF: Attention-Based Multi-Feature Fusion for Binary Vulnerability Detection
Abstract: Binary vulnerability detection remains a significant challenge in modern software security, particularly when software is reused across diverse architectures, compilers, and optimization settings. Variations introduced during compilation lead to substantial differences in binary representations, making it difficult to reliably identify known vulnerabilities at scale. Existing approaches often rely on limited feature representations or struggle to generalize across heterogeneous environments.  This work introduces AMMF, an attention-driven multi-feature fusion approach for cross-architecture binary vulnerability detection. The method combines assembly-level semantic representations with structural features extracted at both basic block and function levels. Instruction semantics are modeled through learned embedding representations, while contextual relationships are captured using a gated recurrent unit enhanced with self-attention. These semantic representations are fused with control-flow and function-level attributes and processed through an attention-augmented convolutional architecture to produce a unified and discriminative function representation.  AMMF employs a two-stage detection strategy. First, a neural similarity model identifies candidate vulnerable functions from large-scale binary corpora. Second, graph-based matching over control-flow structures refines the results to improve precision. This design enhances both scalability and accuracy while reducing false positives.  Experimental results demonstrate strong performance across cross-architecture, cross-compiler, and cross-optimization settings, highlighting the effectiveness of attention-guided representation learning in improving robustness and generalization for binary vulnerability detection.

Title: Make Agents Remember the Right Context at the Right Time
Abstract: Agentic systems are rapidly moving from prototypes to productionbut a critical challenge remains unsolved: memory. In real-world deployments, agents dont fail because they cant reasonthey fail because they retrieve the wrong context, forget important signals, or overload themselves with irrelevant information. This talk dives deep into memory architectures for production-grade agent systems, moving beyond naive vector database + RAG patterns. We introduce a practical framework for designing agent memory across three layers: working memory (context window), retrieval memory , and persistent memory and how to orchestrate them effectively.  Well explore: Dynamic context management: what to keep, compress, or discard Hybrid retrieval strategies combining semantic, structured signals Well also present a reference architecture and real-world failure scenarios, showing how poor memory design leads to hallucinations, cascading agent errors, and cost blowupsand how to fix them.

Title: Building an ML Automoderation System for LLM-Augmented Online Platforms
Abstract: In this session, I'll explain how Social Discovery Group built a production ML automoderation system for user-to-user messaging at scale. The talk covers the full workflow: collecting/aligning chat data with Trust & Safety policies, hybrid models (rules + MLLMs for grey areas), thresholding under extreme imbalance, and routing to reviewer queues with priority scoring.  We'll dive into: - Data prep: sampling rare violations, policy-aligned labels. - Model stack: lightweight classifiers for high-risk (toxicity, spam), MLLMs for nuanced cases (harassment, grooming). - Evaluation: imbalance-aware metrics (precision@low recall), error analysis loops with moderator feedback. - Production: A/B thresholds, monitoring drift, safe rollouts as abuse evolves.  This system processes millions of messages daily, reducing reviewer load by 60% while catching 95%+ of violations. Attendees will get a blueprint for LLM-powered moderation pipelines that balance safety, scale, and false positives  critical for LLM-generated content and agentic interactions.

JobTitle1: Cyber Security and Infrastructure Operations manager
Title: Identity-Centric Security for AI Systems: IAM as Cybersecurity Infrastructure
Abstract: The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs), AI copilots, autonomous agents, APIs, and cloud-native automation has fundamentally expanded the identity attack surface. Modern AI systems rely on complex interactions between users, services, models, plugins, orchestration frameworks, and machine identities, making traditional perimeter-based security increasingly ineffective. As enterprises integrate LLMs into production workflows, identity has emerged as the primary control plane for securing AI-driven infrastructure.  This session explores identity-centric security as a foundational architecture for modern AI ecosystems, reframing identity and access management (IAM) from an administrative capability into critical cybersecurity infrastructure. Rather than focusing on individual tools, the talk examines identity as a structural control governing authentication, authorization, privilege boundaries, and trust relationships across distributed AI systems.  The presentation highlights several infrastructure-level impacts. First, identity-driven least privilege reduces attack surfaces through granular, context-aware, and time-bound access controls for both human and non-human actors. Second, continuous identity verification limits lateral movement and privilege escalation across AI pipelines, APIs, vector databases, orchestration layers, and cloud workloads. Third, centralized identity governance improves resilience by enabling rapid containment, revocation, and recovery when AI systems are compromised. The session also addresses the growing dominance of non-human identities, including AI agents, automation services, and machine-to-machine workloads, which increasingly outnumber human users in modern environments.  By positioning identity as the security boundary for AI ecosystems, this talk provides a strategic framework for building scalable, resilient, and trustworthy LLM-powered systems where identity, not the network perimeter, becomes the foundation of defense.

Title: From ML to Agentic AI: Engineering Reliable LLM Workflows
Abstract: Many organizations adopting LLM-based systems quickly discover that production reliability depends on much more than model quality alone. Retrieval pipelines, orchestration layers, data validation, and operational monitoring often determine whether these systems remain useful at scale.  This session explores the evolution of telecom ITSM ticket routing from traditional machine learning approaches to modern architectures built around Transformers, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and agentic AI workflows.  The talk focuses on the engineering infrastructure required to support reliable LLM operations in production environments. Topics include event-driven pipelines, schema validation, feature management, retrieval orchestration, and strategies for grounding model outputs using real-time knowledge sources.  Attendees will see practical comparisons between earlier TF-IDF and ensemble approaches and newer Transformer-based systems, along with lessons learned from deploying retrieval-driven workflows in operational environments where consistency and maintainability matter.  The session also examines common operational challenges such as retrieval quality degradation, context management, orchestration complexity, and balancing automation with observability across distributed systems.  Rather than treating LLMs as isolated models, this talk focuses on the surrounding engineering systems that make large-scale AI workflows dependable in real-world environments.

Title: Agentic AI Threats and Defenses in Enterprise SASE and Cloud Networks
Abstract: Enterprises are rapidly transitioning from legacy SD WAN infrastructures to Secure Access Service Edge (SASE) architectures that integrate networking, security, and cloud connectivity. While this shift improves scalability and operational agility, it also expands the attack surface across distributed environments. At the same time, Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems capable of autonomous decision making and task execution are emerging as a new class of threat that traditional security controls are not designed to counter.  This session examines how Agentic AI can reshape the enterprise risk landscape and how organizations can design defenses suited for AI enabled adversaries. The discussion draws on a large scale migration of more than 2,300 retail stores from Cisco Viptela to Cisco Meraki SASE. During the migration, exposure points across identity management, policy enforcement, and cloud access were analyzed and mapped to AI driven exploitation scenarios that could accelerate reconnaissance, infiltration, and lateral movement.  To address these risks, layered defense strategies were evaluated using observability platforms such as ThousandEyes and Splunk, combined with proactive anomaly detection and AI assisted remediation workflows. The results showed measurable operational benefits, including a 35% reduction in incident resolution time and a 42% improvement in resilience against service disruptions.  By connecting empirical insights from a real enterprise migration with practical security strategies, this session provides a framework for protecting SASE environments against emerging AI driven threats and building resilient cloud connected networks.

Title: Practical LLM-Driven Predictive Resilience in Multi-Cloud Systems for Real-World Applications
Abstract: The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) is transforming how modern cloud systems are designed, operated, and maintained, particularly in complex multi-cloud environments. While traditional infrastructure resilience relies heavily on reactive monitoring and rule-based automation, real-world deployments increasingly demand intelligent, adaptive systems capable of understanding context, diagnosing issues, and recommending or executing corrective actions. This talk presents a practical approach to integrating LLMs into multi-cloud resilience workflows, focusing on real systems, lessons learned, and production-ready architectures.  We explore how LLMs can augment existing observability pipelines by interpreting heterogeneous telemetry data, logs, and incident reports across cloud providers. By combining LLM capabilities with time-series forecasting and anomaly detection models, teams can build systems that not only predict failures but also generate human-readable insights, remediation suggestions, and automated runbooks. Real-world implementation examples demonstrate how such systems reduce mean time to resolution, improve cross-team collaboration, and simplify debugging in distributed environments.  The session also highlights architectural decisions, including integrating LLMs with Kubernetes-based orchestration, designing provider-agnostic data pipelines, and balancing latency, cost, and reliability. We discuss challenges encountered in production, such as prompt design, hallucination risks, security considerations, and maintaining observability at scale, along with practical solutions that worked and those that did not.  Attendees will gain actionable insights into building and deploying LLM-enhanced resilience systems, including performance tuning strategies, workflow automation techniques, and postmortem-driven improvements. This talk emphasizes real engineering trade-offs and practical takeaways, helping teams move from experimental LLM use to reliable, production-grade systems that improve uptime, reduce operational overhead, and enhance overall system resilience.

Title: Continuous Agentic Deployment: An Open-Source Framework for AI-Powered CI/CD Pipelines
Abstract: LLM agents can generate code, but deploying them reliably in real development workflows requires more than prompting a model. Teams need structured pipelines, observability, retry mechanisms, and clear points where humans stay in control.  In this talk, Denis introduces Continuous Agentic Deployment (CAD)  an open-source framework for integrating AI agents directly into CI/CD pipelines. The framework combines GitHub Actions, prompt libraries, evaluation steps, and cost dashboards to orchestrate specialized agents responsible for issue analysis, code generation, testing, and deployment.  Well walk through a real architecture for running agent-powered pipelines, including patterns for retries, validation steps, human review gates, and metrics for tracking cost and throughput. The session also covers lessons learned from early experiments, including where automation fails and how to design safe fallbacks.  Attendees will leave with a practical blueprint for deploying AI agents in production pipelines while maintaining reliability, observability, and engineering discipline.

Title: Shift-Left Performance Engineering for LAG and LLM Platforms with CI/CD Performance Signatures
Abstract: As organizations adopt large language model (LLM) and retrieval-augmented generation workloads in production, performance engineering can no longer remain a late-stage validation activity. LLM-enabled systems introduce unique challenges, including latency variability, multi-service dependency chains, infrastructure elasticity, and the difficulty of establishing objective release readiness criteria. In these environments, defects discovered late in the lifecycle are costly, slow to diagnose, and disruptive to delivery velocity.  This session presents a practical shift-left performance engineering framework for modern AI and cloud-native platforms, integrating Apache JMeter for workload simulation, Jenkins for CI/CD orchestration, and Dynatrace and Grafana for full-stack observability. At the center of the approach is Performance Signature Analysis, a statistical baseline comparison method that automates pass/fail quality gates and removes subjective interpretation from regression detection. Applied consistently across code changes, this method enables teams to validate system behavior within minutes and detect degradations before they reach production.  The talk focuses on real implementation patterns: self-service Jenkins pipeline templates for developers, automated quality gates for every commit, live Grafana dashboards for test visibility, and Dynatrace Davis AI for root-cause identification across application, API, database, and infrastructure layers. These practices are especially relevant for LLM and AI platforms, where performance regressions may emerge from orchestration logic, downstream services, vector retrieval layers, or infrastructure contention rather than model inference alone.  A case study from an enterprise microservices environment demonstrates measurable outcomes, including reduced production performance incidents, faster response times, increased automated test frequency, and significantly improved mean time to detect. Attendees will leave with practical guidance for embedding repeatable, data-driven performance validation into CI/CD pipelines and building performance-first engineering practices for AI-era systems.

Title: From Acceleration to Assurance: Governing AI Code Assistants in SOX and ICFR Audit Analytics
Abstract: AI code assistants powered by large language models are rapidly reshaping how audit analytics tools are built, enabling teams to generate SQL queries, Python scripts, and testing routines in seconds. While these capabilities promise significant productivity gains, they also introduce critical risks that challenge the integrity of SOX and ICFR compliance processes.  This session examines the dual reality of AI-assisted audit development through evidence-based insights. On one hand, AI can efficiently scaffold common audit proceduressuch as data extraction, Benfords Law analysis, and duplicate payment detectionreducing development time and lowering technical barriers. On the other hand, these gains diminish when tasks require deep accounting logic or complex reasoning, such as ASC 606 revenue recognition or multi-entity reconciliation workflows.  More importantly, security and compliance risks are often underestimated. Studies show that a substantial portion of AI-generated code contains vulnerabilitiesincluding SQL injection risks, hard-coded credentials, and insecure data handlingdirectly threatening the reliability of audit evidence. Compounding the issue, developers frequently exhibit increased confidence in AI-generated outputs despite reduced security quality.  To address this gap, the session introduces a practical governance framework tailored for compliance-driven environments. This includes integrating automated security scanning (Bandit, SonarQube, CodeQL) into CI/CD pipelines, establishing controlled prompt engineering standards for secure and compliant code generation, and applying test-driven development practices to validate AI-generated outputs before they are accepted as audit evidence.  Attendees will gain a clear, actionable approach to leveraging LLM-powered development while preserving the rigor, traceability, and assurance standards required for SOX and ICFR testing.

Title: From Diffusion to Production: How vLLM-Omni Unifies Multimodal Inference
Abstract: Most LLM engineers know autoregressive models inside out, but diffusion transformers play by completely different rules. They don't generate token by token. They denoise. They're compute-bound, not memory-bound. But how do you keep serving LLMs while still generating videos and images in the same inference engine? Is there a way for diffusion to sit inside the same serving pipelines as your LLMs?  vLLM-Omni is the inference engine built for exactly this reality. In this talk, we'll start with just enough diffusion fundamentals  from noise to pixel  the denoising loop, why timesteps matter, and how DiT differs fundamentally from AR models. Then we'll dive into vLLM-Omni's architecture and how it integrates both worlds together: natively disaggregated AR and DiT stages, the OmniRouter dispatch logic, available acceleration for diffusion, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints serving text, images, video, and audio from a single engine.  You'll leave with a clear mental model of where multimodal inference is heading  and what it actually takes to serve it in production.

Title: Building Reliable LLM Systems: Managing Decision Latency and Cognitive Load in the AI Era
Abstract: Large Language Models are accelerating every phase of software delivery from code generation to documentation, testing, and analysis. Yet as execution speeds increase, a new constraint emerges: human decision capacity. In LLM-enabled enterprises, reliability and governance are increasingly limited not by model capability, but by decision latency and cognitive overload.  This talk reframes these human constraints as first-class system properties in AI platform design.  Decision latency appears in model approvals, prompt reviews, data governance checks, safety validations, and incident response loops. When experimentation is fast but ownership and accountability are unclear, AI initiatives accumulate hidden risk and stalled progress.  Cognitive load intensifies as engineers manage model selection, evaluation metrics, hallucination mitigation, prompt engineering, security boundaries, and compliance requirements. As AI increases change frequency, unmanaged complexity leads to burnout and fragile systems.  Using a distributed-systems lens, this session introduces patterns such as explicit decision boundaries, clear AI platform ownership models, policy-as-code guardrails, and opinionated golden paths for LLM adoption.  Attendees will gain practical strategies to convert AI acceleration into sustainable reliability, responsible governance, and high-performing AI engineering teams.

Title: Clean and Controlled AI with API Management
Abstract: Everyone uses AI to improve their organization, but if you are not careful, youll drain your budget, increase energy usage, or create risks you never intended. Do you keep a leash on your AI?  In this session I will demonstrate how to bring solid governance and sustainability into your AI landscape using Azure API Management and Azure AI Foundry. You will learn about practical controls such as content filtering, token-aware throttling, and caching. I will show you when and how to implement these controls to create safer and more sustainable AI systems.  If you want your organization to waste less energy on AI systems while making them safer to use, this talk is for you!

Title: An Advanced Validation Pipeline for Summary Evaluation in High-Stakes Environments
Abstract: In this lightning talk, we delve into the technical intricacies of our comprehensive validation pipeline designed for evaluating the quality of generated summaries in high-stakes environments. We will explore the setup of the pipeline, which integrates traditional metrics such as BERTScore, ROUGE, and BLEU, alongside an innovative "LLM as Judge" method in order to assess summaries across five critical custom dimensions: completeness, conciseness, harmlessness, correctness, and readability. We will discuss the meticulous validation process, including the use of synthetic summaries evaluated by experts and the incorporation of negative control summaries to test robustness. Attendees will gain insights into the technical details of our approach, including the criteria for each evaluation dimension and the scoring system. Furthermore, we will share techniques that led to significant performance improvements, such as reiterating on initial human evaluations and the strategic use of LLMs. We will demonstrate how we measure an agreement between human feedback and the pipeline's assessments, underscoring its reliability and potential for application in high-stakes environments.

Title: Beyond the Context Window: Building a "Mental Model" for Your AI Agents
Abstract: Weve all seen what happens when you give an LLM a single repo: its usually quite successful. But what happens when you give it a distributed system with 200 microservices? It gets lost in the "muddy middle". The bottleneck in AI-driven development isn't code generation; its architectural context.  This talk introduces the Application Architecture Hub - a living, AI-maintained knowledge base designed specifically for agent consumption.   We will explore how to move away from static, stale documentation and toward a system of automated "facets" (APIs, Domain Models, Events, and more) that allow AI agents to troubleshoot, design, and assess impact across your entire ecosystem. We'll explore how this can benefit workflows beyond coding, across the organization.  Attendees will leave with a blueprint for building an ingestion pipeline that keeps their AI agents (and their humans) in sync with the "truth" of the codebase.

Title: Spec Driven Modernization: Modernize your Repository using Spec2Cloud and the GHCP Modernization CLI
Abstract: Every enterprise has it: a sprawling legacy codebase that nobody wants to touch. Hundreds of thousands of lines of code written in frameworks nobody remembers, with business logic buried in layers of technical debt. Traditional modernization is slow, expensive, and risky. What if LLMs could do the heavy lifting  and do it safely, at scale?  In this session, I'll share lessons from real-world agentic code modernization projects  including a large-scale transformation at a major Portuguese financial institution  where we used Spec2Cloud and the GitHub Copilot (GHCP) Modernization CLI to drive spec-driven modernization at scale.  We'll cover:  Spec-driven development: generating machine-readable specifications from legacy code as the contract for safe transformation Spec2Cloud: how it spec-enables your legacy codebase for LLM-driven cloud migration The GHCP Modernization CLI: orchestrating AI agents across analysis, transformation, and validation workflows end-to-end Multi-agent pipelines with RAG over the codebase for grounded, context-aware migrations Evaluation strategies: LLM-as-judge and automated metrics to verify the LLM got it right What worked, what broke, and what we'd do differently This isn't a vendor demo  it's a practitioner's honest account of taking LLMs into production on a mission-critical modernization programme. You'll leave with a realistic picture of where agentic AI genuinely accelerates modernization work, and where you still need human engineers in the loop.

Title: LLM Agents as Co Pilots for Scalable Decision Systems in Production Environments
Abstract: Large Language Models are rapidly evolving from experimental tools into core components of production systems, yet many organizations still struggle to translate their capabilities into reliable, decision oriented applications. While most implementations focus on chat interfaces or isolated automation, the real opportunity lies in using LLM agents as co pilots within complex decision systems that operate at scale.  This session presents a practical approach to embedding LLM driven agents into real world decision workflows, using forecasting and operational planning as a representative use case. The talk introduces a three layer architecture designed for production environments. The first layer focuses on signal aggregation, where structured and unstructured data sources are continuously processed to create a unified context. The second layer leverages LLM agents to reason over this context, generate adaptive forecasts, recommend actions, and explain outcomes in natural language. The third layer enables human interaction, allowing users to query the system, evaluate scenarios, and guide decisions through conversational interfaces.  The session highlights key challenges encountered in deploying such systems, including managing hallucinations, ensuring consistency across decisions, handling latency constraints, and maintaining cost efficiency at scale. It also explores patterns for grounding LLM outputs using enterprise data, orchestrating multi step reasoning, and integrating agents into existing operational workflows without disrupting system stability.  Rather than replacing human expertise, LLM agents augment decision making by reducing cognitive load and enabling faster scenario evaluation. Attendees will gain actionable insights into designing, deploying, and scaling LLM powered decision systems that move beyond experimentation into measurable business impact, along with practical considerations for building reliable and adaptable AI driven architectures in production environments.

Title: Building Real-Time AI Decision Systems with Large Language Models
Abstract: As enterprises adopt large language models across business and engineering workflows, the challenge is no longer just deploying AI models, but integrating them into reliable, real-time decision systems. This session explores how organizations are combining LLMs, analytics, and modern data platforms to support scalable operational intelligence across complex environments.  The talk examines practical implementation challenges including fragmented data sources, governance concerns, infrastructure scalability, and maintaining trust in AI-assisted recommendations. It also discusses how enterprises are moving from static reporting and isolated AI experiments toward continuously evolving systems that combine predictive analytics, streaming data, and LLM-assisted insights.  Drawing from enterprise implementation studies and modernization initiatives, the session will cover architectural approaches for integrating AI models into production workflows while balancing performance, observability, and operational reliability. Additional discussion will focus on how reinforcement learning, retrieval-based systems, and real-time analytics pipelines are being applied to improve responsiveness and decision quality without adding unnecessary system complexity.  This session is intended for AI engineers, software developers, architects, platform teams, and technology leaders interested in practical approaches to building scalable and reliable AI-driven decision systems.

Title: RuntimeGuard-AI: Cryptographic Compliance for High-Risk AI Pipelines
Abstract: RuntimeGuard-AI enables provable EU AI Act compliance for high-risk AI systems in production environments. It instruments the full inference pipelinefrom prompt intake to output validationgenerating tamper-evident, hash-chained records per request. Each record captures executed controls, decision paths, and overseer verification using digital signatures and Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs), allowing regulators to validate compliance without exposing proprietary data. The system supports low-latency operation across NLP, vision, and multi-agent systems, with real-time bias detection and human-in-the-loop escalation. Cryptographic attestations and chain-of-custody tracking ensure verifiable oversight across distributed agents, aligning compliance with modern DevOps observability and auditability practices.

Title: Eliminating Memory Leaks in Spark Streaming with Executor-Level Resource Design
Abstract: Apache Spark Structured Streaming powers many large-scale, real-time data pipelines, including those supporting modern AI and LLM data workflows. However, long-running streaming jobs frequently suffer from memory leaks that undermine stability and reliability. These issues often stem from repeatedly initializing non-serializable resourcessuch as Kafka consumers, network clients, or metrics agentswithin each micro-batch, leading to uncontrolled memory growth and eventual job failure.  This session presents a proven approach to mitigating these challenges using a per-executor singleton resource management pattern. By leveraging techniques such as foreachPartition and mapPartitions, non-serializable resources are instantiated only once per executor JVM and reused across tasks, avoiding redundant allocations and significantly improving memory efficiency.  Drawing from real-world production workloads processing millions of records daily, this pattern demonstrates measurable impact: reduced memory consumption, elimination of garbage collection spikes, and improved stability across extended runtimes. The approach remains resilient during task retries and executor restarts, with no additional serialization overhead.  Attendees will gain a clear understanding of the root causes of memory leaks in streaming systems, practical strategies for managing executor-scoped resources, and best practices for ensuring clean lifecycle management. This talk is especially relevant for engineers building or operating scalable data pipelines that feed AI and LLM systems, where reliability and performance are critical.

Title: Log-Centric Intelligence: Enabling Real-Time Compliance Observability with LLM-Ready Architectures
Abstract: Modern financial ecosystems operate across highly distributed environments where critical compliance data is fragmented among heterogeneous databases, messaging layers, and operational platforms. This fragmentation creates significant challenges for Anti-Money Laundering (AML), sanctions screening, and customer verification workflows that increasingly demand real-time visibility, explainability, and operational intelligence. Traditional reporting approaches depend heavily on cross-system database joins and centralized storage models, introducing latency, scalability limitations, and elevated security exposure. This session presents a practical architecture that transforms distributed logs into a unified operational intelligence layer capable of supporting both real-time compliance monitoring and emerging Large Language Model (LLM) integrations. Instead of treating logs as passive audit artifacts, the proposed design introduces a lightweight wrapper library and a standardized correlation identifier that enable asynchronous event streams from disparate systems to be stitched into a continuous, traceable narrative without requiring physical database consolidation. Attendees will learn how this decoupled architecture powers database-agnostic dashboards, accelerates anomaly detection, and simplifies observability across distributed financial systems. The talk will also explore how structured log intelligence creates an LLM-ready foundation for advanced operational use cases such as natural language compliance investigations, contextual alert summarization, intelligent incident triage, and conversational analytics. Through real-world architectural lessons, this session will discuss implementation tradeoffs, scalability considerations, security implications, and operational outcomes observed while building and evolving the system. Participants will leave with actionable insights into designing resilient, observable, and AI-compatible compliance infrastructures that reduce engineering friction while improving transparency, responsiveness, and decision-making across complex enterprise environments.

Title: LLM-Driven Financial Ledgers: Building Intelligent, Real-Time Accounting Systems at Scale
Abstract: The rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) is reshaping how financial systems are designed, built, and operatedespecially in traditionally rigid domains like General Ledger (GL) systems. This talk presents a practical, architecture-first approach to integrating LLMs into AI-powered financial ledgers, drawing from real-world system design and deployment experiences in the insurance sector. We explore how LLMs augment event-driven architectures built on Apache Kafka by enabling intelligent processing of unstructured financial data, automating journal entry generation, and enhancing reconciliation workflows through contextual understanding.  Beyond theory, this session dives into what actually works in production: combining LLMs with structured ML models (such as LSTM and Random Forests), implementing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for auditability, and addressing latency, cost, and reliability challenges in high-throughput environments. We also discuss lessons learnedfailure modes, prompt engineering pitfalls, and observability strategies that made complex AI systems manageable.  Attendees will gain actionable insights into designing scalable, compliant, and efficient LLM-driven financial platforms, including security considerations and performance tuning. This talk is ideal for engineers and architects looking to move beyond experimentation and build robust, real-world LLM applications in fintech.

Title: From Tooling to Reasoning: Agentic LLMs for Enterprise Dev Productivity
Abstract: Developer productivity in large enterprises is often constrained by system complexity rather than developer capability. In monorepos with thousands of services, engineers spend significant time identifying ownership, tracing failures, and reconstructing missing context. This talk presents a practical architecture leveraging large language models (LLMs) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), Model Context Protocols (MCP), and agentic workflows to address this context deficit.  The approach standardizes enterprise knowledgesuch as service ownership, API contracts, telemetry schemas, and runbooksthrough MCP, enabling LLMs to reason over authoritative internal data instead of relying on generic model knowledge. RAG grounds responses, while agentic workflows coordinate multi-step reasoning across error pattern analysis, code navigation, observability signals, and hypothesis validation.  When integrated into IDEs and CI/CD pipelines, this system reduced time-to-diagnosis for production issues and accelerated onboarding by replacing manual exploration with intent-driven interactions. Enforcing strict context boundaries minimized hallucinations, increasing trust and enabling production adoption.  The session concludes with lessons from real-world deployment, highlighting that context engineeringnot model sophisticationis the key driver of effective LLM systems, and explores future directions for embedding AI reasoning into incident response and change management workflows.

Title: Compliance-by-Construction for Scalable, Privacy-Resilient Credit Apps
Abstract: Co-branded credit card ecosystems operate at the intersection of regulated banking infrastructure and high-volume consumer platforms, where security breaches are common and financial impact is significant. This session presents a compliance-by-construction architecture that embeds regulatory, security, and privacy controls directly into system design while sustaining large-scale transaction processing.  Grounded in economic security modeling, the framework aligns security investments with breach probability, enabling organizations to optimize spending relative to risk exposure. Behavioral compliance research further strengthens governance by incorporating empirically validated predictors of policy adherence, emphasizing the combined role of organizational culture and technical enforcement.  From a systems perspective, the architecture leverages event-driven microservices and advanced logging to handle high-volume, real-time telemetryaddressing the reality that many failures leave no trace in traditional logs. PCI-DSS-aligned micro-segmentation and AI-assisted tokenization techniques reduce computational and memory overhead while preserving strong security and user experience outcomes.  At scale, high-performance networking and distributed transaction protocols support extremely high throughput with microsecond-level latency. These capabilities enable real-time underwriting, fraud detection, and partner integrations without compromising compliance or resilience. The session also explores how AI/LLM-driven components can enhance anomaly detection, observability, and adaptive policy enforcement in regulated environments.

Title: Taming Training-Serving Skew in Production LLM & Ads Ranking Systems
Abstract: Training-serving skew remains one of the most persistent and costly challenges in large-scale machine learning systems, especially in advertising and LLM-powered ranking environments handling billions of inference requests. Even subtle inconsistencies between offline training pipelines and online inference signals can silently degrade model quality, delay issue detection, and increase operational burden.  This talk presents a practitioner-focused framework for systematically identifying, mitigating, and preventing training-serving skew in production systems. We introduce a taxonomy of skew sources grounded in real-world incident patterns, including code divergence, data inconsistencies, temporal gaps, and semantic drift. These categories represent distinct failure modes that require different mitigation and monitoring strategies.  The session compares consistency approaches such as shared transformation libraries, feature logging, manual parity checks, and compiler-driven feature generation. We discuss how deterministic feature computation can eliminate entire classes of discrepancies, while observability techniques improve auditability and accelerate debugging.  We also present a practical detection playbook combining statistical distribution comparison, shadow-mode validation, and snapshot-based regression guards to enable continuous monitoring and faster anomaly detection. Attendees will leave with actionable guidance for building resilient ML and LLM infrastructure that sustains model reliability and performance at scale.

Title: Scaling LLM Data Integration with MCP and GraphQL
Abstract: Large Language Models are rapidly becoming the interface to enterprise systems, yet one major challenge remains: how to connect LLMs to diverse internal data sources without building fragile, custom integrations for every service.  This talk presents a practical architecture that combines the Model Context Protocol (MCP) with GraphQL orchestration to create a scalable, standards-based data layer for AI and LLM-driven systems. Instead of building N×M point-to-point integrations between AI agents and enterprise services, organisations can adopt an N+M protocol model, dramatically reducing integration complexity.  Using real performance evaluations and production implementationsincluding the Apollo MCP Serverthe session shows how this architecture simplifies AI-data connectivity while improving performance. GraphQL enables precise, schema-driven data access, eliminating over-fetching and multi-round trips, resulting in 4060% faster query responses and 3050% lower bandwidth usage for AI agents.  The talk also explains how MCP functions as a universal interface for AI tools, enabling automatic resource discovery, context-aware interactions, and seamless integration across multi-agent LLM systems. When paired with GraphQLs unified data graph, AI applications gain a machine-readable contract that significantly reduces development effort and long-term maintenance.  Attendees will also see how centralized GraphQL resolvers maintain existing authentication, authorization, and audit controlsallowing organizations to scale LLM adoption without compromising security.  Participants will leave with a deployable architectural pattern, performance benchmarks, and migration strategies for integrating LLM applications with enterprise data platforms at production scale.

Title: LLM-Driven Dynamic Product Catalog Integration for Real-Time E-commerce Synchronization
Abstract: Modern e-commerce platforms struggle to maintain accurate and synchronized product information across multiple sales channels, inventory systems, and customer-facing applications. Traditional batch-based catalog updates often introduce inconsistencies, delayed pricing changes, and poor customer experiences. This presentation explores how Large Language Models (LLMs) can enhance dynamic product catalog integration by automating product enrichment, intelligent categorization, and real-time synchronization workflows within modern commerce ecosystems.  The session presents a practical architecture combining event-driven systems with LLM-powered metadata processing to improve catalog accuracy and operational efficiency. It demonstrates how organizations can leverage LLMs for attribute normalization, multilingual content generation, duplicate detection, and contextual product recommendations while maintaining scalable real-time synchronization between ERP systems, marketplaces, and storefronts.  The talk also covers implementation lessons from integrating AI-assisted catalog pipelines into existing commerce infrastructures, including prompt engineering strategies, latency optimization, observability practices, and data governance considerations. Special focus is given to balancing automation with reliability, reducing manual intervention, and improving customer trust through consistent product experiences.  Attendees will gain actionable insights into building production-ready AI-enhanced catalog systems that are scalable, maintainable, and aligned with real-world business requirements.

Title: Building Reliable Infrastructure for LLM Workloads on Kubernetes
Abstract: I will explain what teams need to get right in Kubernetes before LLM services become reliable enough for real-world use, as running LLM workloads in production is not only a model problem but also an infrastructure one. The session will focus on the operational side of LLM platforms, including cluster design, networking, redundancy, secrets handling, and control-plane stability in environments where teams need predictable behavior under load. Attendees will learn which infrastructure assumptions break first when LLM workloads move from experimentation to production, especially in on-prem or tightly controlled environments. Drawing on hands-on experience with Kubernetes networking, BGP, ECMP, kube-router, Ansible, Vault, and etcd, I will show how platform decisions affect service availability, failover behavior, and operational risk. I will also cover practical lessons on reducing fragility, improving observability around platform behavior, and building a stronger foundation for AI services that need to be reliable.

Title: From LLMs to Production: Operationalizing Enterprise AI with MLOps
Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) accelerate enterprise AI adoption, many organizations struggle to move from experimentation to reliable production systems. This session focuses on Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) as the discipline that bridges the gap between model development and enterprise-scale deployment, with specific relevance to LLM-driven applications.  It examines how MLOps integrates machine learning, DevOps, and data engineering practices to enable repeatable, governed, and scalable workflows across the full lifecycle of AI systems, including LLMs. The presentation introduces the MLOps maturity model, showing how organizations evolve from manual, fragmented processes to automated pipelines supporting continuous integration, deployment, and monitoring.  Core architectural components are explored, including data and prompt pipelines, model versioning, deployment automation, and production monitoring. Particular attention is given to operational challenges such as model drift, performance degradation, and compliance considerations that emerge in real-world LLM deployments.  Industry use cases from financial services, retail, and healthcare illustrate how MLOps supports managing production AI systems while maintaining reliability and auditability. The session also highlights governance practices, documentation, and automated monitoring strategies that sustain long-term performance and meet regulatory expectations.  Attendees will gain a practical understanding of how MLOps enables the transition from experimental LLMs to dependable, production-grade enterprise AI systems.

Title: Agile Transformation for Scalable Healthcare IT in Regulated AI Era
Abstract: Healthcare organizations are investing heavily in large-scale IT modernization to support electronic health records, interoperability, analytics, and increasingly AI- and LLM-driven clinical decision support. Yet many initiatives struggle under traditional waterfall models that depend on long, sequential phases and assume stable requirements. In dynamic healthcare environmentswhere clinical workflows, regulatory policies, and operational priorities continuously evolvethis rigidity often results in delayed delivery, misaligned solutions, and increased risk.  This session explores how Agile transformation enables healthcare organizations to deliver complex enterprise IT programs more effectively in the age of AI and LLMs. By shifting from multi-year delivery cycles to short, iterative sprints of 24 weeks, teams can deliver incremental value while incorporating continuous feedback from clinicians, administrators, and compliance stakeholders.  Drawing from large healthcare IT programs that integrate legacy systems, modern platforms, regulatory constraints, and cross-disciplinary teams, the session highlights how Agile practices enhance collaboration across clinicians, developers, data analysts, and compliance experts. This approach enables early validation of workflows such as patient intake, care management, and AI-assisted decision support, reducing design and implementation risks.  The talk also presents a scalable Agile architecture tailored for regulated healthcare environments, spanning experience, application, integration, data, and infrastructure layers. This model supports incremental delivery of capabilitiessuch as provider portals, interoperability APIs, and AI-enabled systemswhile maintaining stability and compliance.

Title: Your AI Has Too Much Access: Deterministic Authorization for Coding Agents
Abstract:
Your coding agent needs better permissions, and so does the code it writes.

AI Agents without explicit boundaries can take actions outside their mandate. A common approach to preventing this is letting the agent decide for itself what's safe. In fact, Anthropic’s analysis of Claude Code's auto-permissions mode reports a 17% false-negative rate on dangerous actions and is upfront about the tradeoff. This is a big limitation in enterprise because Agents are making probabilistic judgments but authorization requires deterministic answers.

This session walks through how you can add explicit, enforceable permission boundaries to your coding agent using SpiceBox, an open-source tool built on SpiceDB. SpiceBox is built on the Google Zanzibar model of authorization which uses Relationship-Based Access Control (ReBAC). This talk explains how this tool secures your agent, and why traditional models such as Role-Based Access Control break down when you need to scope permissions by path, command prefix, or domain. There’s a deep-dive into how the tool can prevent common threat vectors such as prompt injection, data exfiltration, Multi-tenant MCP tool access, and more.

The talk includes a live demo that will show scoping a coding agent to not write outside its allowed paths, and see what network isolation actually looks like in practice. We’ll also show how an agent can request more access and a human can grant it cryptographically. Learnings from this talk could prevent a big data breach!

Title: AI-Driven Profitability Insights: Tesla vs GM in a Transforming Market
Abstract: As industries undergo rapid digital transformation, large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to analyze and interpret complex financial data. This study applies structured financial analysis to compare the profitability performance of Tesla Inc. and General Motors Company, using key ratios derived from published financial statements over an extended period.  Gross profit, operating profit, and net profit ratios are examined to assess financial strength, operational efficiency, and long-term sustainability. The analysis shows that Tesla demonstrates stronger gross profitability, reflecting its innovation-driven business model and premium positioning. However, its operating and net profitability fluctuate considerably, largely due to sustained investments in research, development, and production expansion during its growth phase. In contrast, General Motors exhibits greater stability in operating and net profitability, supported by its manufacturing scale, diversified portfolio, and mature market presence.  Statistical testing confirms that differences in gross profitability are significant, while variations in operating and net profitability are not statistically distinct. The findings highlight how contrasting strategic approachesinnovation-led growth versus scale-driven stabilityshape financial outcomes. For LLM-focused audiences, this study demonstrates how AI-enabled financial interpretation can enhance comparative corporate analysis in data-rich, evolving industries.

Title: AI-Driven Master Data Governance with SAP MDG and Large Language Models
Abstract: Enterprise organizations depend on accurate and trusted master data to support operations, analytics, compliance, and decision-making. However, maintaining consistent master data across distributed systems, regions, and business units remains a major challenge due to siloed applications, duplicate records, and manual governance processes. As organizations accelerate digital transformation initiatives, traditional master data management approaches often struggle to keep pace with growing data complexity and operational demands.  This session explores how SAP Master Data Governance (SAP MDG) can be enhanced using Large Language Models (LLMs) and AI-driven automation to modernize enterprise data governance practices. The presentation demonstrates practical approaches for integrating intelligent capabilities into SAP MDG workflows to improve data quality, automate validations, streamline approvals, and accelerate master data onboarding across enterprise landscapes.  We will discuss real-world implementation scenarios where AI-assisted governance improves material master creation, supplier onboarding, customer data harmonization, and financial master data standardization. The session also covers architecture patterns for integrating LLM-powered services with SAP and non-SAP systems using APIs, web services, and cloud-based AI platforms. Attendees will learn how intelligent recommendations, automated classification, anomaly detection, and natural language-assisted workflows can reduce manual effort while improving governance accuracy and compliance.  In addition, the talk highlights lessons learned from deploying scalable governance frameworks, including workflow optimization, security considerations, auditability, data privacy, and performance tuning in hybrid enterprise environments. We will examine how organizations balance automation with human approvals to maintain governance integrity while increasing operational efficiency.  By combining SAP MDG with modern AI and LLM capabilities, enterprises can establish a more adaptive, intelligent, and scalable master data governance strategy that supports faster business transformation, improved trust in enterprise data, and better decision-making across complex digital ecosystems.

Title: Secure Cross-Account LLM Platforms with AWS PrivateLink for Enterprise AI Governance
Abstract: As organizations scale Large Language Model (LLM) adoption across multiple teams and cloud environments, securing shared AI infrastructure becomes increasingly complex. This session presents a governance-first architecture for securely exposing centralized machine learning and LLM services across AWS accounts using AWS PrivateLink, enabling enterprise AI adoption without relying on public internet connectivity.  The talk focuses on practical implementation strategies for building secure cross-account AI platforms that support internal LLM APIs, inference services, vector databases, and model orchestration layers while maintaining strict security boundaries and operational governance. Attendees will learn how organizations can centralize AI infrastructure and safely provide controlled access to multiple business units, applications, and development teams through private network communication.  Using real-world architectural patterns, the session explores hub-and-spoke governance models, centralized AI platforms, and hybrid deployment approaches designed for scalability, compliance, and operational efficiency. The presentation highlights practical lessons learned around IAM policy design, least-privilege access management, service discovery, request monitoring, distributed tracing, and audit logging across multi-account environments.  The session also covers key operational challenges encountered when running enterprise AI services at scale, including traffic isolation, cost allocation, observability, API throttling, authentication strategies, and performance optimization for high-volume inference workloads. Attendees will gain insight into how PrivateLink-based architectures improve security posture while simplifying AI service consumption for internal teams.  In addition, the talk discusses how this framework supports modern AI workloads such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), serverless inference pipelines, and containerized LLM deployments while remaining adaptable to evolving enterprise governance requirements.  This session is intended for cloud architects, platform engineers, DevOps teams, and AI practitioners looking for practical guidance on building scalable, secure, and enterprise-ready LLM infrastructure in AWS environments.

Title: LLM Copilots for SRE: Transforming Incident Response and Auto-Remediation
Abstract: As digital systems grow increasingly complex, Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) teams must respond to incidents faster and with greater context than ever before. Large Language Models (LLMs) are emerging as powerful copilots that help teams detect, understand, and remediate production issues in real time.  This talk explores how LLM-powered copilots are transforming incident management workflows. Modern AIOps platforms ingest massive volumes of telemetrymetrics, logs, and tracesand apply techniques such as anomaly detection, clustering, and change-point analysis. When paired with LLMs, these signals can be summarized, correlated, and translated into actionable insights for engineers during high-pressure incidents.  Through a real-world case study, we examine an LLM-enabled chatbot integrated with PagerDuty that reduced mean time to detect (MTTD) by 31% by summarizing alerts, querying observability dashboards, and correlating incidents with recent code deployments. Beyond detection, LLMs are also augmenting auto-remediation systems. When combined with orchestration tools like StackStorm and Rundeck, LLMs enable context-aware remediation decisionssuch as traffic rerouting or automated rollbacksresulting in up to 42% fewer repeat incidents.  However, reliability risks remain. LLM hallucinations, automation failures, and governance challenges make human oversight essential. This session discusses practical guardrails, human-in-the-loop workflows, and governance patterns that allow organizations to safely deploy LLM copilots in reliability-critical environments.  Attendees will gain architectural insights and practical strategies for integrating LLM copilots into incident response systems, enabling faster detection, smarter remediation, and more resilient operations.

Title: Building Intelligent E-commerce Systems with LLMs, GenAI, and Autonomous AI Agents
Abstract: The rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) has transformed how intelligent systems are designed, deployed, and scaled in real-world applications. In the e-commerce domain, combining LLMs with Generative AI and autonomous AI agents is enabling a new class of production-ready systems that go beyond traditional automation. This talk focuses on practical implementation strategies, architectural decisions, and lessons learned from building and operating LLM-powered e-commerce solutions.  We explore how LLMs can be integrated into existing pipelines to power conversational search, dynamic product descriptions, personalized recommendations, and customer support automation. Rather than treating LLMs as standalone components, this session demonstrates how they function as orchestration layers within larger systems, interacting with APIs, databases, and business logic through AI agents. We discuss real-world design patterns, including retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), tool-using agents, and prompt engineering workflows that improve reliability and reduce hallucinations.  The talk also highlights key challenges encountered in production, such as latency, cost optimization, observability, and handling unpredictable model outputs. We share practical debugging and monitoring approaches, including prompt versioning, output validation, and feedback loops that enhance system performance over time. Additionally, we examine trade-offs between using hosted APIs versus open-source models, and how these decisions impact scalability and maintainability.  Through concrete examples and case-based insights, attendees will gain a clear understanding of what works, what fails, and how to iterate effectively when building LLM-driven systems. This session is designed for engineers and practitioners who want actionable guidance on deploying LLMs in production environments, improving system robustness, and leveraging AI agents to automate complex workflows in modern e-commerce platforms.

Title: LLM-Powered Network Operations: Intelligent Observability and Automation
Abstract: Enterprise network operations generate thousands of daily alerts (often 2,50010,000) across distributed systems, cloud platforms, and microservices architectures. Much of this signal is redundant or symptomatic of a single root cause, creating alert fatigue and slowing incident response. This session explores how Large Language Models (LLMs), combined with natural language interfaces, real-time telemetry, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), are reshaping infrastructure operations. Instead of manually correlating data across fragmented tools, engineers can interact with systems conversationally and receive context-aware, evidence-based insights. We present a practical architecture for LLM-driven network assistants that integrates platforms such as Splunk, Prometheus, ServiceNow, F5, and NGINX into a unified intelligence layer. The talk demonstrates how LLMs reduce cognitive load by synthesizing operational data, accelerating alert triage, streamlining data gathering, and improving root cause analysiskey phases that typically dominate incident resolution time. Attendees will also learn about a structured trust model for automation that aligns LLM-driven actions with risk levels, enabling safe adoption while preserving human oversight for critical operations. Additionally, we address challenges such as knowledge fragmentation, alert noise, and secure enterprise use of LLMs. By combining LLM reasoning with real-time observability data, organizations can achieve faster diagnostics, improved efficiency, and more consistent incident response across teams.

Title: LLM Enabled Multi Document Correlation for Financial Compliance and Fraud Detection
Abstract: Enterprise financial compliance is increasingly challenged by multi jurisdictional regulations, growing data volumes, and sophisticated fraud patterns that exploit inconsistencies across financial records. Traditional rule based systems and document level NLP approaches remain limited in detecting relationships that span multiple datasets.  This session presents an AI driven multi document correlation framework enhanced with large language models to enable cross document reasoning and fraud detection. The approach combines three key components: a graph based entity correlation engine that connects payroll, tax, procurement, and transactional data; an adaptive probabilistic risk model that aggregates anomaly signals into calibrated risk scores; and a normalization layer that aligns currencies, tax structures, and reporting standards across jurisdictions. Large language models are incorporated to improve semantic understanding, contextual linking, and interpretation of unstructured financial documents.  The framework was evaluated on approximately 3 million anonymized records across four jurisdictions over five years, reflecting real world enterprise conditions. Results demonstrate strong performance with high precision and recall, a significant reduction in false positives compared to rule based systems, and measurable decreases in manual audit workload.  By enabling continuous cross document analysis and learning from audit feedback, this approach shifts compliance from a reactive validation process to a predictive and intelligence driven model. The session will highlight architectural design, evaluation insights, and practical considerations for applying large language models in scalable enterprise compliance systems.

Title: RAG for Data Engineers: What It Is, Where It Fits, and Why Your Metadata Is the Missing Piece
Abstract: Large language models are only as useful as the context you give them. For data engineers, that context is your schema, your lineage graph, your query history, your dbt models — and most teams haven't connected those dots yet. This session is a practical introduction to Retrieval-Augmented Generation from a data engineering lens. We'll cover what RAG actually does under the hood, why it matters more for data teams than most, and the four places it shows up naturally in the data engineering workflow: answering schema questions without digging through a stale catalog, grounding SQL agents in your actual table definitions, giving incident response agents access to historical pipeline context, and surfacing institutional knowledge that currently lives only in senior engineers' heads.

Title: Responsible AI Product Management for Production LLM Lifecycles
Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly moving from experimentation into production systems, but many organizations still manage them using traditional Product Development Lifecycle (PDLC) practices designed for deterministic software. In real-world deployments, LLM systems introduce probabilistic behavior, evolving outputs, model drift, bias risks, limited explainability, and operational uncertainty that standard governance and product management approaches often fail to address.  This session presents a practical Responsible AI Product Management framework for building, deploying, and operating LLM-based products across the PDLC lifecycle. Drawing from real-world product management and governance practices, the talk explains how teams can integrate ethics, compliance, trust, and operational controls into everyday AI delivery workflows without slowing innovation.  The session covers how product managers can translate frameworks such as the OECD AI Principles and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework into actionable lifecycle controls. Topics include defining trust-aware requirements for fairness, explainability, and auditability; embedding compliance-by-design into product planning; and using governance artifacts such as Model Cards and Datasheets for Datasets to improve transparency and accountability.  Attendees will also learn practical approaches for extending validation beyond traditional QA through bias testing, robustness evaluation, scenario-based validation, and continuous monitoring strategies that help detect model drift and changing user behavior after deployment. The talk highlights lessons learned from managing AI systems where reliability, governance, and user trust must evolve alongside the model itself.  This session is designed for product managers, AI practitioners, and engineering leaders looking for practical methods to operationalize Responsible AI in production LLM systems while balancing business goals, regulatory expectations, and long-term system reliability.

Title: The Judge Problem: Why Your Agentic Verifier is Silently Rotting in Production
Abstract: Every agentic system eventually faces the same question: who judges the agent? Most teams bolt on a verifier late, treat it as QA, and watch it collapse under real-world load. This session argues that the verifier is the hardest component to build and the most expensive to get wrong.  Drawing on production work with a bandit-based verification layer routing across Claude, GPT, and Gemini, we will cover:  The Intelligence Inversion: Why verifiers should usually be smarter than the agents they watch.  Systematic Disagreement: How to identify and exploit predictable patterns in LLM-as-judge setups.  Dynamic Routing: Moving beyond simple ensemble voting to sophisticated bandit routing.  Scale Economics: What true verification costs look like when moving from demo to production.  Attendees will leave with a clear framework for building verification layers that are more resilient than the agents they monitor.
